questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon increasing the intensity of a light source,
options: the quantity of photons emitted by the source per unit time increases
the total energy of the emitted photons per unit time increases
photons of higher energy are emitted
photons travelling at a faster speed are emitted
questions.solution:
Intensity of light is defined as the total energy emitted per unit area per unit time.

$I = \dfrac{E}{A \cdot t} = \dfrac{n h u}{A \cdot t}$

where $n$ is the number of photons emitted in time $t$ and $ u$ is the frequency of light.

When the intensity of a given light source is increased, its frequency $ u$ remains constant. Therefore, the number of photons emitted per unit time $\left(\dfrac{n}{t} ight)$ increases.

The total energy of the emitted photons per unit time is the power $P = \dfrac{n h u}{t}$, which also increases as $n$ increases.

The energy of an individual photon is $E = h u$, which depends only on the frequency and remains unchanged.

The speed of a photon in a given medium is constant and does not depend on the intensity of the light source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photon of energy $h\nu$ is absorbed by a free electron of a metal with a work function $\phi < h\nu$.
options: The electron either does not emerge or it emerges with a kinetic energy of $h\nu - \phi$.
The electron is certain to be emitted.
The electron may emerge with a kinetic energy less than $h\nu - \phi$.
The electron is certain to be emitted with a kinetic energy of $h\nu - \phi$.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of an emitted photoelectron is given by $K_{\max} = h u - \phi$.

This maximum kinetic energy corresponds to the electrons emitted from the surface without suffering any collisions.

However, when a photon is absorbed by an electron deeper inside the metal, the electron may suffer collisions with other atoms or electrons before reaching the surface, thereby losing a part of its energy.

As a result, the kinetic energy of the emitted electron can be any value ranging from $0$ to $h u - \phi$. It is also possible that the electron loses so much energy that it does not emerge at all.

Therefore, the electron may emerge with a kinetic energy less than $h u - \phi$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoelectric effect experiment, the photocurrent increases if
options: the intensity of the source is increased
the exposure time is decreased
the exposure time is increased
the intensity of the source is decreased
questions.solution:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident light, assuming the frequency of the incident light is greater than the threshold frequency.

The intensity of light represents the number of photons incident per unit area per unit time.

When the intensity of the source is increased, more photons strike the metal surface per second. This results in the emission of a greater number of photoelectrons per second, thereby increasing the photocurrent.

Photocurrent is a measure of the rate of emission of electrons and is independent of the exposure time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an experiment on the photoelectric effect, if the wavelength of the incident light is doubled,
options: photoelectric emission may or may not occur
the stopping potential will increase
the stopping potential will decrease
photoelectric emission will not occur
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by $K_{\max} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$, where $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal and $V_s$ is the stopping potential such that $K_{\max} = eV_s$.

When the wavelength of the incident light is doubled, the new energy of the incident photon becomes $E' = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{E}{2}$.

If $\dfrac{E}{2} < \Phi$, photoelectric emission will stop. If $\dfrac{E}{2} > \Phi$, photoelectric emission will continue. Thus, photoelectric emission may or may not occur.

If emission continues, the new stopping potential $V_s'$ is given by $eV_s' = \dfrac{E}{2} - \Phi = \dfrac{\Phi + eV_s}{2} - \Phi = \dfrac{eV_s - \Phi}{2}$.

Since $\Phi > 0$, we have $V_s' < \dfrac{V_s}{2} < V_s$. Therefore, the stopping potential will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a photoelectric effect experiment, the collector plate is maintained vertically above the emitter plate. A light source is turned on, and a saturation photocurrent is measured. Following this, a vertically downward electric field is switched on.
options: The photocurrent will increase.
The kinetic energy of the electrons will increase.
The stopping potential will decrease.
The threshold wavelength will increase.
questions.solution:
The collector plate is placed vertically above the emitter plate, so the emitted photoelectrons travel in the upward direction.

When a vertically downward electric field $\vec{E}$ is switched on, the force experienced by an electron of charge $-e$ is $\vec{F} = -e\vec{E}$. Since the electric field is directed downwards, the force on the electrons will be directed upwards.

This upward force acts in the direction of motion of the electrons, causing them to accelerate. As a result, the kinetic energy of the electrons will increase as they move towards the collector plate.

The saturation photocurrent depends only on the intensity of the incident light and the number of photoelectrons emitted per second, so it remains unchanged.

The threshold wavelength is a characteristic property of the emitter material (its work function) and is unaffected by the external electric field.

Because the electrons acquire additional kinetic energy from the electric field, a greater retarding potential would be required to stop them, meaning the magnitude of the stopping potential will increase, not decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The quantum nature of light is supported by the photoelectric effect because
options: the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is determined solely by the light's frequency, and not by its intensity
photoelectrons leave the metal surface immediately, even when it is faintly illuminated
the electric charge of the photoelectrons is quantized
a minimum frequency exists below which no photoelectrons are emitted
questions.solution:
The photoelectric effect provides evidence for the quantum nature of light (photon theory) by explaining phenomena that classical wave theory could not:

According to the photon theory, light consists of discrete energy packets (photons) with energy $E = h u$. The maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by Einstein's photoelectric equation $K_{\max} = h u - \Phi$, which shows it depends solely on the frequency $ u$ and not on the intensity of light.

The emission of photoelectrons is an instantaneous process. A single photon transfers its entire energy to an electron at once, leading to immediate emission even if the light is faintly illuminated. Classical wave theory incorrectly predicts a time delay for energy accumulation.

There exists a minimum threshold frequency $ u_0 = \dfrac{\Phi}{h}$ below which no emission occurs, regardless of the intensity. Wave theory incorrectly predicts that sufficiently intense light of any frequency should eventually cause emission.

The quantization of electric charge is a fundamental property of matter (electrons), not light, and is not related to the quantum nature of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For which of the following scenarios will the heavier of two particles have a shorter de Broglie wavelength? The two particles
options: travel with the same linear momentum
possess the same kinetic energy
have dropped through the same height
travel with the same speed
questions.solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{mv} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$.

For particles with the same linear momentum $p$, the wavelength $\lambda$ is the same regardless of their masses.

For particles with the same kinetic energy $K$, $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$. Since the heavier particle has a larger mass $m$, it will have a shorter de Broglie wavelength.

For particles dropped through the same height $H$, their final speeds are equal, given by $v = \sqrt{2gH}$.

For particles traveling with the same speed $v$, $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{m}$. The heavier particle has a larger mass $m$, hence it will have a shorter de Broglie wavelength.
